turns.content: I'm coming from Java, so I used to rely on IDE auto-formatting with strict rules. But when I try to configure rustfmt.toml in my Rust project, the documentation makes it sound like just a few lines of text, and I don't see how that actually controls something like import ordering or whether formatting is enforced in CI.

You are correct to feel confused because the official docs often only show the basic structure without explaining how specific keys map to behavior. In your rustfmt.toml file, the key style.imports.sort_order controls exactly how imports are arranged, and you can set it to None for alphabetical or LexicographicallySorted to match Java's default behavior. The key style.use_smallvec_control_flow is another example where you decide whether smallvec is used in control flow contexts.
Okay, so if I want strict consistency across my team like our Java build pipeline requires, how do I make sure rustfmt runs automatically and rejects code that isn't formatted during the CI process?

To enforce formatting in your GitHub Actions or other CI tooling, you don't just add a configuration file; you must define the format command as an explicit step using cargo-fmt or the built-in formatter trait integration with your build script. You should also consider setting up a separate rustfmt.toml per edition if you mix stable and nightly targets, since the formatting rules can differ slightly between editions based on the feature flags available at compile time.

This looks like a centralized config file similar to how we used to manage formatting rules in our previous project, but instead of just overriding settings for specific crates it seems to apply globally across the entire workspace. I'm worried this will break our existing codebase if we don't handle the import reordering carefully.

The global application is exactly what makes rustfmt.toml so powerful because it ensures every developer follows the same style rules regardless of who writes the code, which prevents the merge conflicts you saw before. When you run rustfmt on your crate, it will automatically reorder your imports according to the rules in this file and format everything else consistently without requiring manual intervention from each team member.

That sounds too rigid for our team; we need to be able to customize formatting per edition or even per module like we did with conditional compilation flags. If I set different values in rustfmt.toml for the standard library edition versus my custom crate, will that actually override the defaults for specific files without rewriting every single source file?

You can absolutely define separate sections within rustfmt.toml to target specific editions or crates using the workspace metadata, so you only apply strict formatting rules where needed while keeping flexibility elsewhere. This means you don't have to manually refactor your entire codebase; instead, you simply add a new configuration block for your custom crate in that file and let rustfmt handle the rest.

Wait, I thought we had to edit every single source file individually because of how imports are managed? If I just change one setting here will it automatically reformat all existing code without me having to touch each file manually?

Exactly, that is the key benefit you're missing; rustfmt scans your entire directory tree and applies the rules from this single configuration file to every Rust source file at once. You only need to update one line in rustfmt.toml and then run the formatter again to get a consistent baseline for everyone.
